mnemonics.title: Vector Space Axioms
mnemonics.content: CANCAA AIADD: Closure, Associative, Comm, Additive Identity, Additive Inverse, Closure under scalar, Associative scalar, Distributive (2), Scalar Identity. Alternative: 'Our Favorite Cats Use Delicious Ice Cream Daily' - Operation rules to remember.

mnemonics.title: Subspace Test
mnemonics.content: Zero In Sum: Check 0 ∈ W (Zero), closed under Addition, closed under Scalar multiplication. Think 'ZIS - Zero In Sum'.

mnemonics.title: Linear Independence Check
mnemonics.content: L-I-N-E: Linear combination equaling zero implies coefficients are Equal to Zero.

acronyms.expansion: Vector Space - A set with two operations satisfying 10 axioms
Subspace - A subset that is itself a vector space
acronyms.usage: Smaller vector spaces within larger ones

acronyms.expansion: Linearly Independent - No vector can be written as combination of others
acronyms.usage: Key property for basis and dimension

acronyms.expansion: Span - Set of all linear combinations of a set of vectors
acronyms.usage: Describes the subspace generated by vectors

visualTips.visualization: Think of adding vectors as placing the tail of one vector at the head of another (head-to-tail method) - like walking in directions successively
Stretching or shrinking a vector - positive scalars stretch/reverse direction, fractions shrink the vector
If you start at origin and can only travel in certain directions (vectors), the span is every point you can reach by any combination of those walks
A subspace must contain its origin (0,0) - think of it as a 'room' that must have a floor at the origin, and you can't leave the room using addition or scaling

analogies.analogy: Think of a vector space like a playground with specific rules - you can swing (add vectors) and slide (scale), but must follow the playground's safety rules (axioms)

Like a currency system - just as any amount can be made from a few coin denominations, any vector can be made from basis vectors. Different currencies (bases) give different exchange rates (coordinates)
Dimension is like degrees of freedom - a 2D plane lets you move in 2 independent directions (like a flat map), 3D gives up-down too (like real world)

Independence is like having unique information - if one vector points in a completely new direction not captured by others, it's independent. Dependent vectors are redundant - like getting the same news from multiple sources
